source_metadata.title_raw: Linda D. Schiltgen, Esq.  - A-18-135 - August 24, 2018 - Santa Rosa

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 24, 2018

Linda D. Schiltgen, Esq.
Deputy County Counsel
County Administration Center
575 Administration Dr., Room 105A
Santa Rosa, CA 95403

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-18-135

Dear Ms. Schiltgen:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the disclosure
provisions of the Act and not under other prohibitions outside of those contained in the Act, such as
the common law.

     Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTION

     Is the County of Sonoma required to file a Form 801 to report grant funds received from
local nonprofit agencies and a local Native American tribe for purposes of gathering public input
and surveying public priorities to assist the County Regional Parks Department in presenting
recommendations to the County Board of Supervisors?

CONCLUSION

     No. For an official to receive a gift under the Act, the gift must provide the official with
some personal benefit. If a third-party payment strictly benefits the agency as a whole and will
result in no personal benefit to a public official, as is the case here, Form 801 is not required.


____________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-135
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You are Deputy County Counsel for the County of Sonoma. Sonoma County Regional Parks
and local city parks provide a broad variety of experiences for Sonoma County residents and
visitors, including developing open space parks, multiuse bikeways, sports fields, playgrounds,
swimming and boating opportunities, and camping and hiking trails. In addition, the open space
protected by the Regional Park system provides critical water sources, watershed protection,
wildfire protection, and wildlife habitat maintenance. Through partnerships with the Sonoma
County Agricultural Preservation and Open Space District and nonprofit organizations, the Sonoma
County Regional Parks system has been expanding and becoming more popular with residents and
visitors to the County.

To continue offering these services and maintaining the system's aging infrastructure, the
Regional Parks Department has been exploring the possibility of making a recommendation to the
Sonoma County Board of Supervisors to place a one-eighth cent special transaction and use tax on
the November 8, 2018 ballot. Since the spring of 2017, the Regional Parks Department has been
undertaking a community outreach effort to get a deeper understanding of the public's thinking
about parks in Sonoma County and how the public prioritizes park needs relative to other
community needs.

To help offset the cost of the outreach efforts and the survey of public priorities, the
Sonoma County Regional Parks Department has accepted grant donations from the following
sources: (1) $50,000 from the Sonoma County Regional Parks Foundation; (2) $50,000 from the
Federated Indians of Graton Rancheria; (3) $50,000 from Resource Legacy Fund; and (4) $7,000
from Trust for Public Lands. The grant funds were accepted by the leadership at Sonoma County
Regional Parks Department for the public purpose of surveying public opinion to determine the
public's desired priorities.

On June 11, 2018, the Sonoma County Board of Supervisors retroactively authorized the
acceptance of these grants for gathering input from the public, educating the public about the park
system's needs, and developing a recommendation for possible next steps for the Board of
Supervisors to consider. Accordingly, the agency head authorized the acceptance of these grant
funds and authorized the use of these payments for official agency business.

The County now inquires whether it is required to file a Form 801 to report these grant
funds received from local nonprofit agencies and a local Native American tribe for purposes of
gathering public input and surveying public priorities to assist the Regional Parks Department in
presenting recommendations to the Board of Supervisors.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines a gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to
the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received…" (Section 82028.) The Act
prohibits certain public officials from accepting gifts from any single source in a calendar year with
a total value of more than $470. (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2.) Under the Act, an employee
of a local government agency who is designated in the agency's conflict of interest code may not
accept gifts from any single source totaling more than $470 in a calendar year if the employee is

File No. A-18-135
Page No. 3

required to report receiving income or gifts from that source on his or her Statement of Economic
Interests. (Section 89503(c).) In addition, officials who are subject to these gift limits must report
gifts totaling $50 or more in a calendar year from a single source on their Statements of Economic
Interests. (Sections 87207 and 87302.)

Regulation 18944 sets forth circumstances under which a payment made to a government
agency is not treated as a gift to the agency official who receives a personal benefit in its use when
the payment is controlled by the agency and used for agency business.² (See Hahn Advice Letter,
No. A-10-064.) The regulation requires state and local government agencies to disclose these
payments on a Form 801. If a third-party payment strictly benefits the agency as a whole and will
result in no personal benefit to an agency public official, Regulation 18944 does not apply and a
Form 801 is not required. (Frederick Advice Letter, No. I-09-020.)

In this case, the grant funds from the nonprofits and the Native American tribe are provided
to the Regional Parks Department for fulfilling a governmental purpose. (See, e.g., Livingston
Advice Letter, No. A-00-280.) Specifically, the Board of Supervisors authorized the acceptance of
the funds for gathering input from the public, educating the public about the park system's needs
and developing a recommendation for possible next steps for the Board of Supervisors to consider
regarding the special transaction and use ballot measure. These funds do not provide a personal
benefit to any agency official, and such payments are not required to be reported on the Form 801.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

² On January 1, 2012, former Regulation 18944.2 became Regulation 18944 with the substance of the former
regulation wholly incorporated into the new regulation.

recommendations to the County Board of Supervisors?
